name: leather
shortDescription: Prepared leather with a workable grain and the dependable smell of saddlers, cobblers, armorers, and belt makers. It can become boots, straps, packs, scabbards, hinges, covers, and a hundred other goods that need to bend without giving up.
flavorText: “Good leather remembers the animal only as strength.”

name: white thread
shortDescription: White thread wound into a neat supply for mending, hemming, embroidery, and the quiet repairs that keep clothing respectable. Tailors like its clean look, healers like its plainness, and innkeepers buy it when sheets begin telling stories.
flavorText: “White thread hides best where everything is supposed to look honest.”

name: green thread
shortDescription: Green thread dyed for leaf patterns, heraldic trim, festival cloth, and careful repairs on cloaks that spend too much time near woods and weather. It is a small color, but weavers know small colors decide whether work looks finished or merely stitched.
flavorText: “A green seam can make plain cloth remember spring.”

name: yellow thread
shortDescription: Yellow thread bright enough for hems, banners, children’s clothes, temple cloth, and merchant marks that want to be noticed from across a stall. It catches light easily, which makes even modest stitching look a little more expensive.
flavorText: “Gold is for nobles. Yellow thread is for everyone else pretending.”

name: notable red thread
shortDescription: Notable red thread with a rich dye and even twist, favored for seals, trim, festival garments, and the kind of embroidery meant to be noticed before the wearer speaks. It costs more than plain thread because confidence is rarely a neutral color.
flavorText: “Red thread does not mend quietly.”

name: notable blue thread
shortDescription: Notable blue thread spun and dyed for guild marks, noble cuffs, devotional cloth, and refined repairs that should look intentional. Its color holds well under handling, which makes it popular with tailors who prefer customers to return for fashion rather than complaints.
flavorText: “Blue thread gives restraint a little shine.”

name: scarce purple thread
shortDescription: Scarce purple thread dyed in costly baths and sold in careful measures rather than careless spools. Robemakers, banner workers, and ambitious courtiers prize it because a single line of purple can make cheaper cloth behave like rank.
flavorText: “Purple is expensive even when it is thin.”

name: white linen
shortDescription: White linen folded into clean, breathable fabric for shirts, sheets, wrappings, table cloths, and summer garments. It sells well wherever people value neatness, cool skin, and the respectable fiction that stains are only temporary visitors.
flavorText: “Linen is plain until the heat arrives.”

name: green linen
shortDescription: Green linen with a soft vegetable shade suited to travel tunics, festival hangings, garden-house curtains, and quiet household finery. It feels practical enough for common buyers, yet colored enough for tailors to charge with a straight face.
flavorText: “A useful cloth can still want to look alive.”

name: yellow linen
shortDescription: Yellow linen dyed for sunny garments, market awnings, children’s bedding, and cheerful table settings that make simple rooms look warmer. It does not carry the weight of silk, but it brings light to places that cannot afford a window.
flavorText: “Some cloth is bought because winter lasted too long.”

name: notable red linen
shortDescription: Notable red linen with a strong color and smooth weave, used for bold shirts, sash cloth, shrine banners, and feast-day household pieces. It is sturdy enough for use and bright enough for ceremony, which makes it easier to sell twice in the same sentence.
flavorText: “Red linen turns usefulness into announcement.”

name: notable blue linen
shortDescription: Notable blue linen valued for calm color, good drape, and a weave that feels finer than ordinary household cloth. Scribes, guild clerks, sailors’ families, and modest officials all buy it when they want dignity without the trouble of silk.
flavorText: “Blue linen is respectability that breathes.”

name: scarce purple linen
shortDescription: Scarce purple linen, costly enough to be measured twice and cut once with nervous hands. It is used for high-status trims, chapel cloths, noble household pieces, and garments whose owners want rank to look slightly more humble than silk allows.
flavorText: “Purple linen whispers what purple silk shouts.”

name: white silk
shortDescription: White silk with a clean shimmer and a softness that makes merchants lower their voices when unfolding it. Tailors reserve it for veils, linings, formal sleeves, bridal cloth, and any customer willing to pay extra for fabric that seems to glow without trying.
flavorText: “White silk makes even silence look expensive.”

name: green silk
shortDescription: Green silk dyed like deep leaves after rain, prized for court sleeves, dancer scarves, shrine ribbons, and luxurious linings that appear only when the wearer moves. Its value lies in the way color and light trade places across the fold.
flavorText: “Green silk is forest shade taught to flatter.”

name: yellow silk
shortDescription: Yellow silk with a warm, luminous sheen, often bought for festival sashes, noble cushions, temple drapery, and theatrical costumes. It draws the eye quickly, which is useful when a buyer wants sunshine available by the yard.
flavorText: “Yellow silk does not enter a room quietly.”

name: notable red silk
shortDescription: Notable red silk with a rich surface and dramatic fall, suited to noble gowns, officer sashes, stage curtains, and gifts meant to settle arguments before they begin. The dye is deep enough that even a narrow strip looks important.
flavorText: “Red silk is what a coin purse sounds like when it blushes.”

name: notable blue silk
shortDescription: Notable blue silk whose sheen shifts between river dusk and polished sapphire, popular with court tailors, high guilds, and patrons who prefer elegance over noise. It sells best to people who want to look wealthy and thoughtful at the same time.
flavorText: “Blue silk is ambition wearing its indoor voice.”

name: scarce purple silk
shortDescription: Scarce purple silk, rare, costly, and difficult to display without starting gossip. It belongs in royal linings, sacred hangings, mage robes, and locked chests where fabric is treated less like cloth and more like portable authority.
flavorText: “Purple silk is sold by length and remembered by rumor.”

name: blue stone arrow
shortDescription: A blue stone arrow fitted with a hard mineral head and fletching chosen for stable flight. Hunters, scouts, and collectors like its strange color, while practical archers ask whether the point chips before admitting it looks impressive.
flavorText: “Some arrows are made to fly. Others are made to be found afterward.”

name: bomb arrow
shortDescription: A bomb arrow built around a volatile charge, traded only by sellers who know exactly where not to store it. Siege crews, saboteur-hunters, and licensed war bands value its frightening utility, though every careful merchant keeps it far from candles and fools.
flavorText: “The arrow does not need to be accurate if the noise is persuasive enough.”

name: magic holy arrow
shortDescription: A magic holy arrow marked with pale runes, clean fletching, and a blessed point used by temple guards and monster hunters who prefer their tools approved by someone pious. It carries more paperwork than ordinary ammunition, but buyers rarely complain when darkness is the target.
flavorText: “Faith travels faster with feathers.”

name: magic arrow
shortDescription: A magic arrow humming faintly with general enchantment, useful to archers who want reliability against foes, locks, wards, or bad luck that ordinary shafts cannot impress. Its exact charm varies by maker, which keeps appraisers busy and customers hopeful.
flavorText: “A plain arrow asks the bow for help. A magic one brings its own argument.”

name: magic fire arrow
shortDescription: A magic fire arrow sealed with heat-bright charmwork and handled with the respect due to anything eager to become flame. It is prized for signal shots, siege work, and monster hunting, though warehouses prefer it stored where insurance clerks cannot see it.
flavorText: “Fire arrows make poor secrets.”

name: heartwood arrow
shortDescription: A heartwood arrow cut from dense, old timber and balanced for a confident draw. Druids, rangers, and traditional fletchers prize its steady grain, claiming it flies truer because the tree spent so many years learning patience.
flavorText: “Old wood remembers how to stand against wind.”

name: skull arrow
shortDescription: A skull arrow decorated with a small bone-like head or grim charm, favored by raiders, stage duelists, curse sellers, and dramatic hunters with theatrical taste. Its trade value rises sharply when fear matters as much as function.
flavorText: “Some fletchers understand that a warning can have feathers.”

name: trident arrow
shortDescription: A trident arrow with a forked head designed to catch, tear, or strike with a wider bite than a normal point. Fishers, marsh fighters, and specialist archers buy it for situations where a clean puncture is less useful than control.
flavorText: “Three points ask fewer questions than one.”

name: vine arrow
shortDescription: A vine arrow wrapped in green charmwork and living-looking tendrils, sold to hunters, wardens, and hedge-mages who need a shot that can snare as well as strike. It must be kept dry, but not too dry, which annoys quartermasters wonderfully.
flavorText: “The best vine arrows continue the conversation after landing.”

name: wood arrow
shortDescription: A plain wood arrow with honest shaft, common fletching, and a point meant for practice, hunting, patrol work, or cheap readiness. It lacks glamor, but armies and villages both know that simple ammunition wins by being available.
flavorText: “A quiver is built from ordinary decisions.”

name: large axe
shortDescription: A large axe with a heavy head and long handle, suited to timber work, gate breaking, and buyers who believe every problem needs more swing. It is awkward in tight rooms but very persuasive outdoors, especially near stubborn wood.
flavorText: “A large axe is subtle only from far away.”

name: sharpened battle axe
shortDescription: A sharpened battle axe honed to a bright edge and balanced for hard use rather than display. Mercenary companies inspect the bevel before the decoration, while cautious merchants wrap it twice before letting anyone gesture with it indoors.
flavorText: “Sharpness is the cheapest enchantment.”

name: battle axe
shortDescription: A battle axe of practical military make, heavy enough to command respect and plain enough to be bought in numbers. It belongs on racks, wagons, training fields, and contracts where durability matters more than heroic poetry.
flavorText: “A battle axe does not need a legend to make space.”

name: gold infused battle axe
shortDescription: A gold infused battle axe with metalwork brightened by decorative inlay and a touch of noble extravagance. Captains buy it for champions, collectors buy it for display, and soldiers argue about whether a beautiful axe is heavier than a plain one.
flavorText: “Gold on an axe is still gold, but the axe keeps its own opinion.”

name: wide battle axe
shortDescription: A wide battle axe with a broad crescent head and a presence that makes shield-makers sigh. Its enlarged blade gives it menace, weight, and a strong silhouette, which matters to warriors who want opponents to reconsider before coming closer.
flavorText: “A wide axe spends half its work before the first swing.”

name: strong battle axe
shortDescription: A strong battle axe reinforced at haft, socket, and edge for long campaigning and rough handling. It is not the lightest tool on the rack, but buyers pay for confidence when replacement shops are three kingdoms away.
flavorText: “Strength is weight that has justified itself.”

name: ceremonial battle axe
shortDescription: A ceremonial battle axe polished for oaths, parades, hall guards, and ancestral displays where symbolism must look capable. It may still be dangerous, but its true work is making authority appear older, sharper, and better dressed.
flavorText: “Ceremony prefers its steel clean.”

name: fancy battle axe
shortDescription: A fancy battle axe dressed with carved haft, bright fittings, and enough flourish to satisfy a warrior with courtly ambitions. It sells to duelists, nobles, collectors, and anyone who insists practical equipment should also accept compliments.
flavorText: “A fancy axe still has to answer plain questions.”

name: big axe
shortDescription: A big axe that favors raw reach and weight over polite balance, made for strong arms, thick timber, and dramatic demonstrations at market stalls. It is easy to admire, harder to carry, and impossible to ignore when leaned against a wall.
flavorText: “Big axes sell to people who measure courage by handle length.”

name: big double axe
shortDescription: A big double axe with two broad blades and a center-heavy stance, impressive enough for champions, guards, and statues that need something serious to hold. Its maintenance cost is doubled, but so is its ability to dominate a conversation.
flavorText: “Two edges make one very loud promise.”

name: blessed blade of purity
shortDescription: The blessed blade of purity carries shrine-polished metal, clean inscriptions, and a reputation for being judged as much as wielded. Priests, monster hunters, and collectors seek it for work where symbolism and sharpness are expected to agree.
flavorText: “Purity is easy to claim and difficult to keep polished.”

name: broad axe
shortDescription: A broad axe with a wide working blade, useful for hewing timber as well as looking severe on a weapon rack. Builders, foresters, and militia quartermasters all understand its appeal: it turns large problems into smaller, flatter ones.
flavorText: “A broad axe believes every shape is negotiable.”

name: ceremonial broad axe
shortDescription: A ceremonial broad axe with polished metal, decorative cuts, and a handle meant to be seen as often as gripped. It appears in guild rites, clan halls, and official displays where honest labor is dressed in public dignity.
flavorText: “Even ceremony needs a tool with shoulders.”

name: fancy broad axe
shortDescription: A fancy broad axe made with ornamented fittings and a carefully finished blade, sold to wealthy foresters, decorated guards, and collectors who appreciate tools that look too proud for ordinary chopping. It is practical enough to use and pretty enough not to.
flavorText: “Fine carving makes the handle no less heavy.”

name: small broad axe
shortDescription: A small broad axe with a compact head for close work, timber shaping, camp use, and craftspeople who need control more than spectacle. It lacks the swagger of larger axes, but wagon builders and roofers know how useful restraint can be.
flavorText: “Small axes get invited where large ones only pose.”

name: fancy small broad axe
shortDescription: A fancy small broad axe with decorative trim and a surprisingly serious working edge. It sells well to guild masters, caravan officers, and careful nobles who want a tool compact enough to carry yet polished enough to explain itself at dinner.
flavorText: “Elegance is easier when the blade fits in a belt loop.”

name: dull broad axe
shortDescription: A dull broad axe whose edge has seen better care, still useful for training, rough splitting, or buyers planning to sharpen it themselves. It is cheaper than pride, heavier than excuses, and often bought by people who insist they enjoy restoration work.
flavorText: “A dull axe is either a bargain or a confession.”

name: strong broad axe
shortDescription: A strong broad axe reinforced for hard timber, harsh weather, and long work away from proper repair benches. Its thick socket and sturdy haft make it popular with frontier crews who would rather carry weight than carry regrets.
flavorText: “The best broad axe survives the tree and the owner.”

name: double axe
shortDescription: A double axe with blades on both sides of the haft, built for symmetry, intimidation, and heavy practice yards where mistakes become memorable. It demands space, balance, and a buyer who understands that twice the edge means twice the upkeep.
flavorText: “A double axe gives no side the privilege of being harmless.”

name: executioner axe
shortDescription: An executioner axe of stark design, heavy-headed and ceremonial in the grim way official tools can be. It is purchased by magistrates, collectors, and theater companies alike, though each buyer gives a different reason for wanting it wrapped before delivery.
flavorText: “Some axes are sharpened for law, not lumber.”

name: executioner axe
shortDescription: A second executioner axe pattern with a broader face and more formal bearing, made for processions, court storage, or grim hall displays. Its market is narrow but reliable, because institutions that need such things prefer replacements before scandals.
flavorText: “When a tool becomes a symbol, someone always orders a spare.”
